Fill missing values in gridded objects

Source: R/fill.R
fill.Rd

`fill()` replaces missing values in matrices, arrays, and supported gridded package classes by interpolating from neighbouring values.

Usage

fill(x, ...)

# Default S3 method
fill(x, method = "akima", control = list(), ...)

# S3 method for class 'array'
fill(x, method = "akima", control = list(), ...)

# S3 method for class 'grid'
fill(x, method = "akima", control = list(), ...)

# S3 method for class 'gts'
fill(x, method = "akima", control = list(), ...)

Arguments

x

An object to fill. Supported inputs include:

  • a numeric matrix;

  • a numeric array whose first two dimensions are spatial;

  • a `grid` object;

  • a `gts` object.

...

Additional arguments passed to the underlying interpolation machinery.

method

Interpolation method used to fill missing values. In the current implementation, only `"akima"` should be considered supported.

control

A named list of control options. Supported entries include:

`mask`

Optional mask used to identify cells that should not trigger filling. If all non-masked cells are already complete, the input is returned unchanged.

`keep_range`

Logical; if `TRUE`, interpolated values outside the observed range of the original data are discarded and remain `NA`.

`create_psi`

Logical; for `grid` objects, controls whether a missing `psi` grid should be created. By default, this is enabled for regular grids.

`padding`

Currently accepted but not used by the shared implementation.

Value

An object of the same general type as `x`:

matrix input

A matrix with missing values replaced where possible.

array input

An array of the same dimensions as `x`, with filling applied independently to each spatial layer.

`grid` input

A `grid` object, potentially with a completed `psi` component.

`gts` input

A `gts` object with its grid and data component filled, subject to the grid mask when present.

Details

For plain matrices, interpolation is performed on the matrix index space using row and column positions. For higher-dimensional arrays, filling is applied independently to each layer beyond the first two dimensions. For `grid` and `gts` objects, the method also updates associated spatial grid components.

The default method fills missing values in a matrix by interpolating over the matrix row and column indices. Existing non-missing values are left unchanged.

For arrays, `fill()` is applied independently over all trailing dimensions, assuming that the first two dimensions represent space.

For `grid` objects, the function first calls `interp_grid()` and then ensures that the `psi` grid is available. For regular grids, `psi` is constructed by extending the longitude and latitude boundaries and averaging adjacent cell corners. For irregular grids, missing `psi` coordinates are padded, filled, and then smoothed with `roll()`.

For `gts` objects, the function first fills the associated grid, then fills the data array stored in `x$x`. If a grid mask is available, it is used during filling and re-applied afterwards with `.mask_correction()`.

See also

[interpolate()]

Examples

if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{
x <- volcano
x[10:15, 12:18] <- NA

xf <- fill(x)
} # }
